10.1016/j.jmp.2017.09.006
The classical way to represent the (non-stochastic) preference structure of an economic agent on a set of alternatives is by means of a binary relation satisfying suitable order properties, which are usually forms of transitivity and/or completeness. Preorders, semiorders  (Luce, 1956; Pirlot & P.Vincke, 1997) , and interval orders  (Fishburn, 1970, 1985)  are the binary relations that are often used for the modelization of preference structures, due to their intrinsic properties: see  (Aleskerov, Bouyssou, & Monjardet, 2007; Pirlot & P.Vincke, 1997)  and references therein.

A very recent approach to preference modeling employs instead a pair of interconnected binary relations on the same set of alternatives. This bi-preference approach has the advantage of allowing a more flexible modelization of an economic agent’s (or a set of economic agents’) preference structure in several scenarios. The two preference relations are nested into each other, and are connected by (economically and psychologically) meaningful properties. The main feature of a bi-preference structure is that the two “core properties” of transitivity and completeness are not required to fully hold for both relations, instead they are suitably spread over the combination of the two relations.

NaP-preferences  (necessary and possible preferences) belong to the family of bi-preferences. Originally, NaP-preferences were introduced in the field of  Multiple Criteria Decision Aid , in the process of constructing a new methodology called  Robust Ordinal Regression  
                      (Greco, Mousseau, & Słowiński, 2008) . However, the axiomatization of this bi-preference structure only came a few years later  (Giarlotta & Greco, 2013) . Even more recently,NaP-preferences have been studied from several perspectives:

–

generalizations of NaP-preferences by uniform types of bi-preferences  Giarlotta and Watson (2017b) ;

necessary and possible  hesitant fuzzy sets  – in the sense of  Torra (2010)  – to model fuzzy decision problems in a collective setting  (Alcantud & Giarlotta, 2017) .

Formally, a NaP-preference is a pair of nested reflexive relations on a set of alternatives such that the smaller component is transitive, the larger is complete, and the two components jointly satisfy theproperties of transitive coherence and mixed completeness. The smaller component is the  necessary preference , and codifies the part of a preference structure that is deemed to be at the very core of the “mental attitude” of the economic agent(s): in fact, it collects all relationships among alternatives that  must  happen. The larger component is the  possible preference , and codifies the “environment” of a preference structure: in fact, it collects all relationships among alternatives that  may  happen. The properties of transitive coherence and mixed completeness are required to hold in order to make the transition between the two components smooth. The naturalness of this bi-preference structure is witnessed by its characterization: a pair of binary relation is aNaP-preference if and only if there exists a family of total preorders, called a  resolution , whose intersection and union give, respectively, the two components (see  Giarlotta & Greco, 2013 ).

As customary in preference theory, one ought to study both the asymmetric and the symmetric counterparts of the notion ofNaP-preference. The analysis of the former has already been carried out in  Giarlotta (2015) . Here we undertake the study of the symmetric counterpart of a NaP-preference, called a NaP-indifference  : this is a pair of reflexive and symmetric relations on the same set of alternatives such that the necessary component is transitive, and the possible component is a transitively coherent enlargement of the former. Thus, a NaP-indifference codifies similarity of alternatives in two forms: “strong” (or “mental”), represented by an equivalence relation, and “weak” (or “behavioral”), represented by a suitable symmetric extension of the core relation. Again, the naturalness of this notion is witnessed by its characterization: a pair of symmetric relations is a NaP-indifference if and only if there exists a family of equivalence relations, called a  resolution , whose intersection and union give the two components (see  Theorem 3.4 ).

We present several examples of NaP-indifferences, and link resolutions of NaP-preferences to those of NaP-indifferences. In particular, we show that natural notions of similarity of items derived from an observed choice behavior – according to Samuelson’sclassical approach of  revealed preference theory  
                      (Samuelson, 1938)  – yield NaP-indifferences on the underlying set of alternatives. Further, we classify NaP-indifferences in two classes, depending on whether they are canonically induced byNaP-preferences or not: we call  derived  the first, and  primitive  the second. The topic of primitive NaP-indifferences turns out to be connected to the primitivity of a single symmetric binary relation, and has therefore a graph-theoretic flavor. Maybe surprisingly, many NaP-indifferences turn out to be primitive, a fact that witnesses the non-redundancy of our analysis.

The paper is organized as follows. In Section  2  we recall the notion of a NaP-preference, and mention some basic results. In Section  3  we introduce the notion of a NaP-indifference, prove a characterization, and give several examples; in particular, we emphasize the links with similarity relations in revealed preference theory. In Section  4  we study the two classes of NaP-indifferences, derived and primitive.

In this section, we summarize some – old and new – facts on NaP-preferences. This preliminary overview of the topic is useful to put further developments of the theory of NaP-preferences in the right perspective. The main references for NaP-preferences are  (Giarlotta & Greco, 2013)  for the seminal paper, and  (Alcantud & Giarlotta, 2017; Cerreia-Vioglio et al., 2017; Giarlotta, 2014, 2015; Giarlotta & Watson, 2017c)  for further developments.

Clearly, any weak preference is the (disjoint) union of its strict preference and its indifference. A weak preference

                            ≿

                         . In fact, both the Ferrers property and semi-transitivity imply quasi-transitivity (and completeness as well), and quasi-transitivity implies acyclicity. Then, a weak preference is called

(1) a  suborder  if it is acyclic,

(2) a  quasi-preorder  if it is quasi-transitive,

(3) a  preorder  if it is transitive,

(4) a  partial order  if it is an antisymmetric preorder,

(5) an  interval order  if it is Ferrers,

(6) a  semiorder  if it is Ferrers and semitransitive,

(7) a  total preorder  if it is a complete preorder (or a transitive semiorder), and

(8) a  linear order  if it is an antisymmetric total preorder (or a complete partial order).

The asymmetric part of a preorder (respectively, a complete preorder, and a linear order) is called a  strict partial order  (respectively, a  strict weak order , and a  strict linear order ).                                         I 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  
                                  ≔ 
                                  
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  ∖ 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               . A NaP-preference is  normalized  if its necessary preference is a partial order: in this case, we denote it by  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ≽ 
                                           
                                           
                                              N 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              ≿ 
                                           
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                               . In what follows,  
                                  
                                     NaP 
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is the family of all NaP-preferences on  
                                  X 
                               , and  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           NaP 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        nor 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                the subfamily of all normalized NaP-preferences.

The axioms of a NaP-preference have a natural interpretation in the realm of decision theory. In fact,  
                            
                               
                                  X 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                          can be written as the disjoint union  
                            
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  N 
                               
                            
                            
                            ∪ 
                            
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  G 
                               
                            
                            
                            ∪ 
                            
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  I 
                               
                            
                            
                         , where  
                            
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  N 
                               
                            
                            ∪ 
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  I 
                               
                            
                            
                          is the “total information” provided by an economic agent (or a set of economic agents) on  
                            X 
                         , and the preference gap  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  G 
                               
                            
                            
                          represents a gray area of “indecisiveness”. More specifically,  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  N 
                               
                            
                            
                          models the positive/necessary part of a single-agent (or multiple-agent) decision structure, and  
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  I 
                               
                            
                            
                          the negative/impossible part of it. 
                            2 
                          
                         
                            2 
                            We refer the reader to  Giarlotta (2014)  and  Giarlotta and Greco (2013)  for an extended discussion on the point. 
                         The next remark lists several immediate consequences of the definition of a NaP-preference, which shed some light on its semantics. 
                            3 
                          
                         
                            3 
                            Although these facts are known (see  Giarlotta, 2015; Giarlotta & Greco, 2013 ), we recall them here for the reader’s convenience.

Remark 2.3 
                            Let  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ≿

                                     ) 
                                  
                                be a NaP-preference on

                                     Property  (TC)  does not guarantee the transitivity of  
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≿ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                        . On the other hand, the combination of the properties of a NaP-preference yield that

                                                 ≿

                                         is a total quasi-preorder, and so  
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≻ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                         is a strict partial order).

                                        ; further, differently from the gap,

                                         is antisymmetric (respectively, complete), then configuration (i) (respectively, (iv)) never appears. 
                                  
                                  
                                     (5) 
                                     The six configurations of  Fig. 1  use two “layers of preference” to model different kinds of relationships between alternatives. Configuration (i) describes a  core indifference  between  
                                           x 
                                         and  
                                           y 
                                        , which is an indifference at the necessary layer, maintained at the possible layer. Configuration (ii) describes a  strong preference  of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                        , which is characterized by a strict preference of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                         at the necessary layer, along with no preference of  
                                           y 
                                         over  
                                           x 
                                         at both layers. Configuration (iii) describes a  balanced preference  of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                        , which is composed of a strict preference of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                         at the necessary layer, compensated by a weak preference of  
                                           y 
                                         over  
                                           x 
                                         at the possible layer. Finally, configuration (iv) describes an ambiguous situation between  
                                           x 
                                         and  
                                           y 
                                        , which is read as  incomparability  at the necessary level, and  indecisiveness  at the possible level. The six configurations of  Fig. 1  use two “layers of preference” to model different kinds of relationships between alternatives. Configuration (i) describes a  core indifference  between  
                                           x 
                                         and  
                                           y 
                                        , which is an indifference at the necessary layer, maintained at the possible layer. Configuration (ii) describes a  strong preference  of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                        , which is characterized by a strict preference of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                         at the necessary layer, along with no preference of  
                                           y 
                                         over  
                                           x 
                                         at both layers. Configuration (iii) describes a  balanced preference  of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                        , which is composed of a strict preference of  
                                           x 
                                         over  
                                           y 
                                         at the necessary layer, compensated by a weak preference of  
                                           y 
                                         over  
                                           x 
                                         at the possible layer. Finally, configuration (iv) describes an ambiguous situation between  
                                           x 
                                         and  
                                           y 
                                        , which is read as  incomparability  at the necessary level, and  indecisiveness  at the possible level.                                   ′ 
                               
                            
                          – in place of mixed completeness  (MC)  and transitive coherence  (TC) .

                                                 z 
                                              
                                              ) 
                                           
                                         
                                        implies 
                                        
                                           
                                           x

                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                           z 
                                           
                                        
                                        .

≿ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 N 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        is a preorder;

≿ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                           
                                         
                                        includes 
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≿

                                        includes 
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 ≻

≿ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 P 
                                              
                                           
                                         
                                        is complete;

for all 
                                        
                                           x

                                           ∈ 
                                           X

Proof 
                            For necessity, assume that  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ≿ 
                                           
                                           
                                              N 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              ≿ 
                                           
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is a NaP-preference on  
                                  X 
                               , that is, properties  (C) - (E) - (MC) - (TC)  hold. Property  (PC)  is an immediate consequence of properties  (E)  and  (MC)  of a NaP-preference. To prove that  (E) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                  
                                holds, assume that  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  X 
                                are such that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               , hence  
                                  ¬ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≿ 
                                        
                                        
                                           P 
                                        
                                     
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               . Property  (MC)  entails  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               . On the other hand,  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                  
                                fails, since otherwise the hypothesis would imply  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                               , a contradiction. It follows that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  
                                holds, as claimed. Finally, we prove property  (TC) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                               . By symmetry, it suffices to show that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                implies  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                  
                               . Let  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  , 
                                  z 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                                be such that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                  
                               . Transitive coherence readily yields  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                               . If  
                                  z 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                were to hold, then we would get  
                                  z 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                               , hence  
                                  z 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                again by transitive coherence, a contradiction. It follows that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                  
                                holds, as claimed. 
                            Conversely, assume that properties  (C) - (E) - (PC) - (E) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                  
                               - (TC) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                hold for

                                              ≿ 
                                           
                                           
                                              N

                                              ≿ 
                                           
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                               . To prove  (MC) , let  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  X 
                                be such that  
                                  ¬ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≿ 
                                        
                                        
                                           P 
                                        
                                     
                                     y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               . Since  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                is complete by  (PC) , it follows that  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                  
                               , hence  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                using  (E)

                               . Thus  (MC)  holds. Finally, we prove transitive coherence. Toward a contradiction, assume that  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  , 
                                  z 
                                  ∈ 
                                  X 
                                are such that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                and  
                                  ¬ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     
                                        
                                           ≿ 
                                        
                                        
                                           P 
                                        
                                     
                                     z 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               . Property  (PC)  readily yields  
                                  z 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  
                               , whence  
                                  z 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                by property  (TC) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                               . However, this is impossible. The proof that  
                                  x

                                  y

                                        P

                                is similar. This completes the proof that

                                              N 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              ≿ 
                                           
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is a NaP-preference.

The next example shows that the description provided by  Lemma 2.4  is minimal, in the following sense: under the assumption that the two basic properties  (C)  and  (E)  hold, each of the remaining three properties is independent of the other two.

Properties  (E) 
                         
                            
                               
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                            
                          and  (TC) 
                         
                            
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          in  Lemma 2.4  describe the characteristic features of the strict possible preference  
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                            
                         : this relation is stronger than the strict necessary preference  
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  N 
                               
                            
                            
                          insofar as it models a situation of preference in which no compensation in the opposite sense is allowed (see configurations (ii) and (ii) 
                            
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          vs. configurations (iii) and (iii) 
                            
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          in  Fig. 1 ). In fact, not only  
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                            
                          is a strict partial order included in  
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  N 
                               
                            
                            
                         , but also it enjoys a property of mixed transitivity whenever combined with a (weak) necessary preference.

Another useful aspect of  Lemma 2.4  is that it singles out property  (PC) , which requires the completeness of the possible component. 
                            5 
                          
                         
                            5 
                            
                               (PC)  stands for “ possible completeness” . 
                         As a consequence, it is natural to think of developing a general theory of  uniform bi-preferences , in which the extended component may well be incomplete, but the other features of a NaP-preference are preserved, either fully or partially  (Giarlotta & Watson, 2017b) .

The next result is a neat order-theoretic characterization of a NaP-preference (see  Giarlotta & Greco, 2013 ).

Theorem 2.6  can be read in two dual ways: descriptive and normative. From a  descriptive  point of view, assume that we are provided with a family  
                            T 
                          of total preorders on a set  
                            X 
                          of alternatives, which represents, e.g., the preference structures of a set of economic agents on a specific decision problem. The NaP-preference  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          induced by this resolution can then be interpreted as a “universal-existential synthesis” of the primitive structures: the necessary component  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  N 
                               
                            
                            
                          codifies the preference judgements on which all the actors of the decision process agree, and the possible component  
                            
                               
                                  ≿ 
                               
                               
                                  P 
                               
                            
                            
                          those judgements displayed by at least one actor.

Dually, from a  normative  point of view, assume that we are provided with a NaP-preference  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          on a set  
                            X 
                          of alternatives. This structure can be interpreted as a set of “universal-existential rules” concerning a certain decision problem, by which a hypothetical set of economic agents should abide. Then any resolution  
                            T 
                          for  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        P 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          represents a family of preference structures that respect those  ex-ante  rules.

Following standard practice in preference theory, in  Giarlotta (2015)  the author defines the asymmetric counterpart of a NaP-preference. It turns out (see  Theorems 2.9 and 2.10  ) that this notion is closely related to that of a normalized NaP-preference.

We conclude this preliminary section by mentioning a recent result about normalized NaP-preferences, which, in view of  Theorems 2.9 and 2.10 , can be adapted to strict NaP-preferences. To that end, first we introduce the notion of a well-graded family of pairs of binary relations, which extends to a bi-preference setting the original notion due to  Doignon and Falmagne (1997) . 
                            11

The well-gradedness of a family  
                            F 
                          of bi-preferences basically ensures a smooth transition from any element  
                            
                               ( 
                               R 
                               , 
                               S 
                               ) 
                            
                            ∈ 
                            F 
                          to any different element  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                            ∈ 
                            F 
                         : this transition is done via a minimal sequence of bi-preferences in  
                            F 
                         , which transforms  
                            R 
                          into  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            S 
                          into  
                            
                               
                                  S 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          by changing – that is, eliminating or adding – one edge (i.e., an ordered pair) a time. The next result, proved in  Giarlotta and Watson (2017c) , paves the way toward a theory of stochastic evolution of NaP-preference structures, similarly to what has been done for mono-preference structures  (Falmagne, 1996, 1997; Falmagne & Doignon, 1988, 1997, 2011) .

Theorem 2.12 
                            
                               The family of all normalized NaP-preferences on a finite set is well-graded.

The family of all normalized NaP-preferences on a finite set is well-graded.

In this section, we introduce the symmetric counterpart of a NaP-preference, called a NaP-indifference. We present several examples and prove a characterization. We also link NaP-indifferences to similarity relations in revealed preference theory.

To start, we give a name to reflexive and symmetric binary relations on a set.

Definition 3.1 
                            A  similarity relation  on  
                                  X 
                                is a reflexive and symmetric binary relation  
                                  ≡ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                               . Equivalently, a similarity on  
                                  X 
                                is the set of edges of an undirected graph on  
                                  X 
                                (having all loops).

A  similarity relation  on  
                                  X 
                                is a reflexive and symmetric binary relation  
                                  ≡ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                               . Equivalently, a similarity on  
                                  X 
                                is the set of edges of an undirected graph on  
                                  X 
                                (having all loops).

In this paper we study suitably interconnected pairs of similarity relations on the same ground set, defined as follows:

Definition 3.2 
                            A pair

The next example collects a few simple instances of NaP-indifferences.

Example 3.3 
                            Let  
                                  X 
                                be a nonempty set, and  
                                  Δ

                                     X 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  =

                                        , 
                                        x 
                                        )

                                     x 
                                     ∈ 
                                     X 
                                     } 
                                  
                                the diagonal of  
                                  X 
                               . The following are NaP-indifferences on  
                                  X 
                               : 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     (i) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 Δ 
                                                 
                                                    ( 
                                                    X 
                                                    ) 
                                                 
                                                 , 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       X 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       2 
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        , the  trivial  NaP-indifference on  
                                           X 
                                        ; 
                                  
                                  
                                     (ii) 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 Δ 
                                                 
                                                    ( 
                                                    X 
                                                    ) 
                                                 
                                                 , 
                                                 S 
                                              
                                           
                                        , where  
                                           S 
                                         is any similarity relation on  
                                           X 
                                        ;

                                                 E 
                                                 , 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       X 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       2 
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        , where  
                                           E 
                                         is any equivalence relation on  
                                           X 
                                        ;

                                                       E

                                                 E

                                           X

                            Thus, NaP-indifferences extend similarity relations (by (ii)), as well as equivalence relations (by (iii), and, more generally, (iv)).

Similarly to NaP-preferences (cf.  Theorems 2.6 and 2.10 ),NaP-indifferences can be characterized by the existence of a suitable family of binary relations. 
                            12 
                          
                         
                            12 
                            However, contrary to  Theorems 2.6 and 2.10 , the Axiom of Choice is not needed to prove this characterization.

Theorem 3.4 
                            
                               A pair

                               of binary relations on 
                               
                                  X

                                  E 
                                
                               of equivalence relations on

                               such that

Proof 
                            Let  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              ≡ 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          N 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              ≡ 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          P 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                be a pair of binary relations on  
                                  X 
                               . For necessity, assume that there exists a nonempty set  
                                  E 
                                of equivalence relations on  
                                  X 
                                such that the equalities  
                                  
                                  ⋂ 
                                  E 
                                  = 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    N 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                  ⋃ 
                                  E 
                                  = 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    P 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                hold. Properties  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           (C) 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                  
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           (E) 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                  
                                are immediate, since the intersection of equivalence relations is still an equivalence relation, and the union of symmetric relations is still symmetric. To prove  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           (TC) 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                  
                               , let  
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  , 
                                  z 
                                  ∈ 
                                  X 
                                be such that  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    N 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    P 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                  
                               . Thus, we have  
                                  x 
                                  ≡ 
                                  y 
                                for each  
                                  ≡ 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  E 
                               , and  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                for some  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  E

                            For sufficiency, assume that properties

                                be a pair of binary relations on
