As for  Theorems 2.6 and 2.10 ,  Theorem 3.4  has a natural economic interpretation, from two (dual) perspectives: descriptive and normative. Let us elaborate on the point. Assume that  
                            M 
                          is a group of economic agents, who are facing a specific decision problem on a set  
                            X 
                          of alternatives. We consider two different scenarios concerning their preference structures, which can be either (1) predetermined, or (2) to be determined.

In scenario (1), assume that all agents’ preference structures are modeled as preorders on a set  
                            X 
                          of alternatives. Thus the symmetric parts of these preorders give rise to a family  
                            E 
                          of equivalence relations on  
                            X 
                         . According to  Theorem 3.4 , we can associate to  
                            E 
                          a NaP-indifference  
                            
                               
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    N 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    P 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               
                            
                          on  
                            X 
                         , which provides a compact description of the aggregated similarity of alternatives as expressed by the group  
                            M 
                         . In fact, the necessary indifference  
                            
                               
                                  ≡ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              N 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          describes similarities on which every agent agrees, whereas the possible indifference  
                            
                               
                                  ≡ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          comprises similarities displayed by at least one agent. 
                            13 
                          
                         
                            13 
                            Or, equivalently, the impossible preference  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    I 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                describes similarities that are rejected by every agent. 
                         In this sense, a NaP-indifference can be viewed as a “universal-existential synthesis” of the similarity judgements expressed by the whole group  
                            M 
                         , and so it has a  descriptive  nature.

Conversely, in scenario (2), assume that agents’ preference structures are to be determined according to some specific goals. To that end, a synthetic description of a possible “similarity structure” of the problem at hand is presented to agents, in the form of a NaP-indifference  
                            
                               
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                              0 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    N 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                              0 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    P 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               
                            
                          on the set  
                            X 
                         . This NaP-indifference has the following semantics:  
                            
                               
                                  ≡ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              N 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          is the very core of the relation of similarity among alternatives, whereas  
                            
                               
                                  ≡ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              I 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          (the complement of  
                            
                               
                                  ≡ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              P 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          in  
                            
                               
                                  X 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                         ) comprises all similarities that can never happen. Each agent  
                            i 
                            ∈ 
                            M 
                          is then supposed to establish his own similarity structure in the form of an equivalence relation  
                            
                               
                                  ≡ 
                               
                               
                                  
                                  i 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              N 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                          on the set  
                            X 
                         , staying as close as possible to the “rule” provided by  
                            
                               
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                              0 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    N

                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                              0 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    P 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               
                            
                         . 
                            14 
                          
                         
                            14 
                            More generally, each agent may establish its own similarity relation without any requirement of transitivity, and then one can take the transitive closure of this relation. 
                         The output of this process is a family  
                            
                               
                                  E 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                          of equivalence relations on  
                            X 
                          (indexed on  
                            M 
                         ). Now  Theorem 3.4  yields a new NaP-indifference  
                            
                               
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡

                                                    N

                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    P 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                               
                            
                         , which is again presented to agents. This process can be iterated until a reasonable compromise is achieved, and a “good” family  
                            
                               
                                  E 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                          ( 
                            n 
                            ≥ 
                            1 
                         ) of equivalence relations on  
                            X 
                          defined. Successively, agents’ strict preferences can be assessed coherently with the indifference structures arising from this procedure. Thus, in this scenario, aNaP-indifference has a  normative  flavor, acting as a sort of “compelling guide” in the process of forming agents’ similarity judgements.

Here we establish the connection of NaP-indifferences with the topic of similarity relations in the field of individual choice theory. In fact, we shall show that suitable pairs of similarity relations associated to an arbitrary choice space form a NaP-indifference. For the sake of simplicity, in this section we restrict our analysis to the case in which the ground set is finite. 
                            15 
                          
                         
                            15 
                            Most of the results presented in this section hold under no assumption of finiteness. However, since our objective is simply to show how NaP-indifferences naturally arise in the field of revealed preference theory, we limit the analysis to the finite case, avoiding some technicalities that may distract the reader from the main goal.

To start, we recall the basic terminology in individual choice theory. A  (finite) choice space  is a pair  
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               c 
                               ) 
                            
                         , where  
                            X 
                          is a finite nonempty set, and  
                            c 
                            : 
                            Ω 
                            → 
                            Ω 
                          is a map on the family  
                            Ω 
                          of all nonempty subsets of  
                            X 
                          with the property that  
                            c 
                            
                               ( 
                               A 
                               ) 
                            
                            ⊆ 
                            A 
                          for each  
                            A 
                            ∈ 
                            Ω 
                         . The family  
                            Ω 
                          is the  choice domain , a nonempty set  
                            A 
                            ∈ 
                            Ω 
                          is a  menu , and an element  
                            x 
                            ∈ 
                            A 
                          is an  item . The  choice set 
                         
                            c 
                            
                               ( 
                               A 
                               ) 
                            
                          collects all items of  
                            A 
                          that are deemed “selectable” by the economic agent.

According to Samuelson’s classical approach of  revealed preference theory , 
                            16 
                          
                         
                            16 
                            See  Samuelson, 1938  for the seminal paper, and  (Arrow, 1959; Hansson, 1968; Houthakker, 1950; Richter, 1966; Sen, 1971)  for some classical contributions to the topic. See also the recent paper  (Cantone et al., 2016)  for an axiomatic development of the theory, according to the transitive structure of the relation of revealed preference. 
                         preferences can be derived from choices: an economic agent’s choice behavior is observed, and her preference structure is inferred. The derived preference is modeled by a suitable binary relation derived from the primitive choice, and is called  revealed preference . Formally, given a choice space

                            c

                         .) In particular, a choice is  transitively rationalizable  if it is rationalizable and its revealed preference is transitive. 
                            17 
                          
                         
                            17 
                            Similarly, it is  Ferrers rationalizable  (resp.  semitransitively rationalizable ) if it is rationalizable and its revealed preference is Ferrers (resp. semitransitive). More generally, a choice is

It is apparent that only the strict part  
                            
                               
                                  ≻ 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          of the relation of revealed preference is relevant for the retrieval of the primitive choice. Here we are interested in the symmetric part  
                            
                               
                                  ∼ 
                               
                               
                                  c 
                               
                            
                          of this relation – the revealed indifference – which is supposed to codify a form of similarity of behavior of alternatives. More generally, by a relation of  revealed similarity  on a choice space  
                            
                               ( 
                               X 
                               , 
                               c 
                               )

                            X 
                         , which connects to each other those alternatives that appear to be somehow interchangeable according to the agent’s choice behavior represented by  
                            c 
                         . The next definition recalls some similarity relations introduced in the literature on individual choice theory.

However, this weaker condition is justified by the authors’ goal, which consists of developing an alternative theory of revealed preferences, based on the construction of a transitive but possibly incomplete binary relation. To that end, Eliaz and Ok work under the assumption that the observed choice behavior displays a form of consistency codified by  WARNI

                                           The notion of “trace” plays a main role in the theory of semiorders  (Luce, 1956)  and interval orders  (Fishburn, 1970) . Sometimes, the trace of a preference relation is defined in a different way, using the strict preference as primitive relation. However, under suitable conditions, the two definitions are equivalent. In particular, it is well-known that the trace of an interval order is a preorder, and the trace of a semiorder is a total preorder (regardless of the way the trace is defined). Semiorders have recently been universally characterized in terms of the existence of alternative forms of traces, called  sliced traces  
                                              (Giarlotta & Watson, 2016) .

To that end, Eliaz and Ok work under the assumption that the observed choice behavior displays a form of consistency codified by  WARNI

Revealed behavioral indifference

                                           19 
                                           The notion of “trace” plays a main role in the theory of semiorders  (Luce, 1956)  and interval orders  (Fishburn, 1970) . Sometimes, the trace of a preference relation is defined in a different way, using the strict preference as primitive relation. However, under suitable conditions, the two definitions are equivalent. In particular, it is well-known that the trace of an interval order is a preorder, and the trace of a semiorder is a total preorder (regardless of the way the trace is defined). Semiorders have recently been universally characterized in terms of the existence of alternative forms of traces, called  sliced traces  
                                              (Giarlotta & Watson, 2016) .

NaP-indifferences naturally arise from choice spaces. In fact, below we shall show that all possible nested pairs of relations of revealed similarity (given in  Definition 3.5 ) turn out to be NaP-indifferences. To that end, we need the following result, which is interesting on its own. 
                            22 
                          
                         
                            22 
                            In  Eliaz and Ok (2006) , the authors implicitly show (in the proof of Theorem 1) that revealed universal indifference is an equivalence relation. However, their result holds under the assumption that the choice satisfies  WARNI  (see Footnote 18). This is quite restrictive, since  WARNI  implies that the choice is quasi-transitively rationalizable. Instead,  Theorem 3.7  holds regardless of any form of rationalizability (and also under no assumption of finiteness).

Theorem 3.7 
                            
                               Revealed universal indifference is an equivalence relation.

Revealed universal indifference is an equivalence relation.

Proof

Corollary 3.8  has a natural interpretation in the field of choice theory, according to either a descriptive or a normative approach: see the discussion following  Theorem 3.4 .

Remark 3.9 
                            The proof of  Corollary 3.8  goes through if, for the first three pairs of binary relations, we put any “congruence relation” on a choice space in place of revealed indiscernibility. (See  Cantone et al., 2017  for the notion of a congruence relation on a choice space: revealed indiscernibility is the maximum congruence relation on a choice space.)

The proof of  Corollary 3.8  goes through if, for the first three pairs of binary relations, we put any “congruence relation” on a choice space in place of revealed indiscernibility. (See  Cantone et al., 2017  for the notion of a congruence relation on a choice space: revealed indiscernibility is the maximum congruence relation on a choice space.)

In this final section, we turn to the analysis of the way in which NaP-preferences arise. We show that there are two categories of NaP-indifferences: (i) “derived”, which are canonically induced by NaP-preferences; and (ii) “primitive”, which cannot be derived from any NaP-preference. It will turn out that mostNaP-indifferences are primitive, and so the related theory is independent of that of NaP-preferences (although the two theories do have an obvious relationship).

To start, we show that NaP-preferences canonically induceNaP-indifferences by taking the symmetric part of their two components.

Lemma 4.1 
                            
                               For each NaP-preference 
                               
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              ≿ 
                                           
                                           
                                              N

                                                          P 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                
                               is a NaP-indifference.

In this subsection we prove some properties of derivedNaP-indifferences, whereas the next one is devoted to the study of the category of primitive NaP-indifferences. We start our analysis by defining a new notion of resolution, which is the symmetric counterpart of that related to NaP-preferences (see  Definition 2.7 ).

The previous example shows that not every resolution for a NaP-preference gives rise to a resolution for the derivedNaP-indifference. However, the same example also suggests that if the primitive resolution is “enriched” with suitable total preorders, then the symmetric family canonically derived from it becomes a resolution for the derived NaP-indifference. Below we show that this fact holds true in general, that is, an arbitrary NaP-preference has a resolution whose symmetric part is a resolution for the derived NaP-indifference.

Definition 4.6 
                            A resolution for a NaP-preference is called  separating  if it satisfies  Theorem 4.5 .

A resolution for a NaP-preference is called  separating  if it satisfies  Theorem 4.5 .

According to Lemma 3.7 in  Giarlotta and Greco (2013) , the family of resolutions for a NaP-preference is a join-semilattice 
                            24 
                          
                         
                            24 
                            A  join-semilattice  is a poset such that any doubleton has a least upper bound in it. 
                         with a unique maximum element, which is obtained by taking the union of all resolutions. Therefore,  Theorem 4.5  has the following immediate consequence:

Corollary 4.7 
                            
                               The maximum resolution for a NaP-preference is separating.

The maximum resolution for a NaP-preference is separating.

In view of  Lemma 4.1 , one may wonder whether the notion of NaP-indifference is a simple derivation of that of NaP-preference, that is, any NaP-indifference always arises as the symmetric part of a NaP-preference. As a matter of fact, if the latter fact were to hold, then the notion of NaP-indifference would be redundant. However, the answer to the above question is negative, in the following (strong) sense:

(i) 
                               the existence of primitive NaP-indifferences goes well beyond the interaction of two binary relations, being connected to the internal structure of a single binary relation; 
                            
                            
                               (ii) 
                               in the finite case, the larger the size of the ground set is, the higher the likelihood of (randomly) obtaining a primitive NaP-indifference becomes.

the existence of primitive NaP-indifferences goes well beyond the interaction of two binary relations, being connected to the internal structure of a single binary relation;

in the finite case, the larger the size of the ground set is, the higher the likelihood of (randomly) obtaining a primitive NaP-indifference becomes.

In this paper we only deal with (i). In fact, it turns out the derivability/primitivity of a NaP-indifference only depends on its second component, and has nothing to do with the first. Specifically, we shall prove that suitable reflexive and symmetric binary relations are “primitive”, in the sense that they fail to be the indifference derived from a total quasi-preorder. As a consequence, since the possible component of a NaP-preference is a total quasi-preorder, it will follow that some (in fact, most) NaP-indifferences are primitive. (Indeed, it can be shown that – due to some graph-theoretic results – the majority of NaP-indifferences on a finite set happen to be primitive; in particular, (ii) holds.)

Definition 4.8 
                            A similarity relation  
                                  ≡ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                is  primitive  if there is no total quasi-preorder  
                                  ≿ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  ≡ 
                                is equal to the indifference  
                                  ∼ 
                                derived from  
                                  ≿ 
                                  
                               ; otherwise,  
                                  ≡ 
                                is  derived . Equivalently,  
                                  ≡ 
                                is primitive whenever there is no strict partial order  
                                  ≻ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  ≻ 
                                is disjoint from  
                                  ≡ 
                                  
                               , and the union of  
                                  ≻ 
                                and  
                                  ≡ 
                                is complete. (Dually,  
                                  ≡ 
                                is derived if it is the incomparability relation of a strict partial order on  
                                  X 
                               , adjoined with the diagonal of  
                                  X 
                               .)

A similarity relation  
                                  ≡ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                is  primitive  if there is no total quasi-preorder  
                                  ≿ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  ≡

                                  ∼ 
                                derived from  
                                  ≿

                                  ≡

                                  ≻

                                  ≡

                                  X

                                  X

The primitivity of a similarity relation can be restated in terms of graph theory as follows.

Definition 4.9 
                            A  comparability graph  is an undirected graph  
                                  G 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     E 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                (where  
                                  X 
                                is the set of vertices, and  
                                  E 
                                the set of edges) with an underlying strict partial order  
                                  < 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  
                                     { 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     y 
                                     } 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  E 
                                if and only if either  
                                  x 
                                  < 
                                  y 
                                or  
                                  y 
                                  < 
                                  x 
                                holds. Whenever the underlying strict partial order  
                                  < 
                                is fixed, we call  
                                  G 
                                the comparability graph  associated  to  
                                  < 
                               . The complement of a comparability graph is an  incomparability graph . Then a similarity  
                                  ≡ 
                                on a set  
                                  X 
                                is primitive whenever there is no strict partial order  
                                  < 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                such that the incomparability graph associated to  
                                  < 
                                is equal to  
                                  ≡ 
                                (minus the diagonal of  
                                  X 
                               ).

A  comparability graph  is an undirected graph  
                                  G 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     , 
                                     E 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                (where  
                                  X 
                                is the set of vertices, and  
                                  E 
                                the set of edges) with an underlying strict partial order  
                                  < 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  
                                     { 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     y 
                                     } 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  E 
                                if and only if either  
                                  x 
                                  < 
                                  y 
                                or  
                                  y 
                                  < 
                                  x 
                                holds. Whenever the underlying strict partial order  
                                  < 
                                is fixed, we call  
                                  G 
                                the comparability graph  associated  to  
                                  < 
                               . The complement of a comparability graph is an  incomparability graph . Then a similarity  
                                  ≡ 
                                on a set  
                                  X 
                                is primitive whenever there is no strict partial order  
                                  < 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                such that the incomparability graph associated to  
                                  < 
                                is equal to  
                                  ≡ 
                                (minus the diagonal of  
                                  X 
                               ).

There is a vast literature on comparability graphs: see the classical results by  Gallai (1967) ,  Ghouilà-Houri (1962) ,  Gilmore and Hoffman (1964) , etc. (See also chapter 3 of Trotter’s book  Trotter, 1992  for more recent references.) By  Lemma 4.11  , it will turn out that the primitivity/derivability of a NaP-indifference is strictly linked to the notion of (in)comparability graph.

The connection between the primitivity of a similarity relation and that of a NaP-indifference is rather simple:

Lemma 4.11 
                            
                               A NaP-indifference is primitive if and only if so is its possible indifference.

A NaP-indifference is primitive if and only if so is its possible indifference.

Proof 
                            For sufficiency, observe that the possible component of a NaP-preference is a total quasi-preorder (see  Remark 2.3  (2) or  Lemma 2.4 ). Thus, a NaP-indifference  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              ≡ 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          N 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              ≡ 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          P 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                such that  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    P 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                is primitive must be primitive. 
                            Conversely, assume that  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              ≡ 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          N 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        ,

                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                follows. The case  
                                  x 
                                  
                                     
                                        ∼ 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    N 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  y 
                                  
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                  z 
                                can be handled similarly. This proves that  
                                  
                                     
                                        ≿ 
                                     
                                     
                                        N 
                                     
                                  
                                is a preorder.

The extension property  (E)  follows from the extension property  (E) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        ≡

                                              ≡ 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          P 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               . Finally, properties  (E) 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                     
                                        ≻ 
                                     
                                  
                                and  (PC)  are obvious, whereas property  (TC)

By  Lemma 4.11 , it suffices to deal with a single similarity relation. We aim at providing a characterization of primitive similarity relations under suitable conditions. In view of the strong links with the theory of comparability graphs, we start by introducing some useful terminology (often borrowed from graph theory).

                                  =

                                  ≿

As a consequence of  Theorem 4.14 , we identify a class of primitive similarity relations.

Corollary 4.15 
                            
                               A similarity relation with full extent is primitive if and only if its extent cannot be partitioned into two cliques.

A similarity relation with full extent is primitive if and only if its extent cannot be partitioned into two cliques.

Proof 
                            Sufficiency is an immediate consequence of  Theorem 4.14 . To prove necessity, we argue by contrapositive. Let  
                                  ≡ 
                                be a similarity relation on  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  E 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     ≡ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  X 
                               . Assume that  
                                  X 
                                can be partitioned into (at most) two cliques, say,  
                                  X 
                                  = 
                                  A 
                                  ∪ 
                                  B 
                               . For each  
                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                                and  
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  B 
                                such that  
                                  ¬ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ≡ 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               , set  
                                  a 
                                  ≻ 
                                  b 
                               . We claim that the relation  
                                  ≿ 
                                  
                               , obtained as the union of  
                                  ≻ 
                                and  
                                  ≡ 
                                  
                               , is a total quasi-preorder on  
                                  X 
                               : this will show that  
                                  ≡ 
                                is derived from  
                                  ≿ 
                                  
                               , thus completing the proof. Completeness is obvious by construction. To prove that  
                                  ≿ 
                                is quasi-transitive, observe that we can never have a sequence of strict preferences of the type  
                                  a 
                                  ≻ 
                                  c 
                                  ≻ 
                                  b 
                               , since we would get  
                                  c 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                               , a contradiction.  
                               
                                  ■

Sufficiency is an immediate consequence of  Theorem 4.14 . To prove necessity, we argue by contrapositive. Let  
                                  ≡ 
                                be a similarity relation on  
                                  X 
                                such that  
                                  E 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     ≡ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  X 
                               . Assume that  
                                  X 
                                can be partitioned into (at most) two cliques, say,  
                                  X 
                                  = 
                                  A 
                                  ∪ 
                                  B 
                               . For each  
                                  a 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                                and  
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  B 
                                such that  
                                  ¬ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ≡ 
                                     b 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               , set  
                                  a 
                                  ≻ 
                                  b 
                               . We claim that the relation  
                                  ≿ 
                                  
                               , obtained as the union of  
                                  ≻ 
                                and  
                                  ≡ 
                                  
                               , is a total quasi-preorder on  
                                  X 
                               : this will show that  
                                  ≡ 
                                is derived from  
                                  ≿ 
                                  
                               , thus completing the proof. Completeness is obvious by construction. To prove that  
                                  ≿ 
                                is quasi-transitive, observe that we can never have a sequence of strict preferences of the type  
                                  a 
                                  ≻ 
                                  c 
                                  ≻ 
                                  b 
                               , since we would get  
                                  c 
                                  ∈ 
                                  A 
                                  ∩ 
                                  B 
                               , a contradiction.  
                               
                                  ■

In the language of graph theory,  Corollary 4.15  – which appears to be new, as well as independent of the main results in comparability graphs mentioned before – can be restated as follows:  If every dissimilarity set 
                         
                            
                               
                                  D 
                               
                               
                                  ≡ 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               a 
                               ) 
                            
                          
                         is path-connected, then 
                         
                            X 
                          
                         can be partitioned into two cliques if and only if the graph (induced by 
                         
                            ≡ 
                         
                         ) is an incomparability graph.  The following consequence of  Corollary 4.15  is known, but it is usually proved in a different way. 
                            26 
                          
                         
                            26 
                            
                               Corollary 4.16  is an easy consequence of a theorem by  Gilmore and Hoffman (1964)  : indeed, for any set of size at least  
                                  5 
                               , consider the similarity relation that is a  
                                  5 
                               -cycle, and argue that the resulting relation cannot be derived (since in the complement graph there is an odd-length path using the five pentagon vertices and having no triangular chord). We thank the managing editor Jean-Paul Doignon for pointing out this alternative proof, as well as some other relationships between results on comparability graphs and those on primitive similarities.

Corollary 4.16 
                            
                               For all 
                               
                                  n 
                                  ≥ 
                                  5 
                               
                               , there is a primitive similarity relation on a finite set of size 
                               
                                  n 
                               
                               .

For all 
                               
                                  n 
                                  ≥ 
                                  5 
                               
                               , there is a primitive similarity relation on a finite set of size 
                               
                                  n 
                               
                               .

Proof 
                            Let  
                                  X 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     … 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                     } 
                                  
                                be a set of size  
                                  n 
                                  ≥ 
                                  5 
                               . Arrange the elements of  
                                  X 
                                in an  
                                  n 
                               -gon, and define a similarity relation  
                                  ≡ 
                                on  
                                  X 
                                by adding to  
                                  Δ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     X 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                all symmetric pairs corresponding to the sides of the polygon. Each dissimilarity set  
                                  
                                     
                                        D 
                                     
                                     
                                        ≡ 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                is composed of all elements of  
                                  X 
                               , except  
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                and its two adjacent vertices; in particular, it is path-connected. Thus  
                                  ≡ 
                                has full extent. Since  
                                  n 
                                  ≥ 
                                  5 
                               ,  
                                  X 
                                cannot be partitioned into two cliques, whence  
                                  ≡ 
                                is primitive by  Corollary 4.15 .  
                               
                                  ■

Remark 4.17 
                            
                               Corollary 4.16  holds in the following stronger form:  The fraction of the similarity relations on an 
                               
                                  n 
                               
                               -element set that are primitive approaches one as 
                               
                                  n 
                                
                               approaches infinity.  This fact follows from the asymptotic enumeration on the number of partial orders on a finite set, given by  Kleitman and Rothschild (1975) .

Corollary 4.16  holds in the following stronger form:  The fraction of the similarity relations on an 
                               
                                  n 
                               
                               -element set that are primitive approaches one as 
                               
                                  n 
                                
                               approaches infinity.  This fact follows from the asymptotic enumeration on the number of partial orders on a finite set, given by  Kleitman and Rothschild (1975) .

The analysis of the primitivity of a similarity relation entails the following consequence, which is connected to our original goal of proving the existence of primitive NaP-indifferences.

Corollary 4.18 
                            
                               For each 
                               
                                  n 
                                  ≥ 
                                  5 
                               
                               , there is a primitive NaP-indifference on a finite set of size 
                               
                                  n 
                               
                               .

For each 
                               
                                  n 
                                  ≥ 
                                  5 
                               
                               , there is a primitive NaP-indifference on a finite set of size 
                               
                                  n 
                               
                               .

Proof 
                            The claim readily follows from  Corollary 4.16  and  Lemma 4.11 .  
                               
                                  ■

The claim readily follows from  Corollary 4.16  and  Lemma 4.11 .  
                               
                                  ■

Corollary 4.18  shows that the notion of NaP-indifference is independent of that of NaP-preference. Therefore, a more general theory of bi-preference structures – in which either transitive coherence  (TC)  or mixed completeness  (MC)  is relaxed – appears possible.

The authors thank Salvatore Greco and Roman Słowiński for some discussions on the notion of NaP-indifference. They are also grateful to the managing editor Jean-Paul Doignon and two anonymous referees for their useful comments and suggestions, which improved the quality of the paper.                    FIR-2014 BCAEA3 ”.
